did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep neural networks (VGG16, VGG19, Xception, ResNet50, InceptionV3, DenseNet121) and ensemble learning methods for skin lesion classification (dermatological image classification) on the ISIC2017 and ISIC2018 datasets. It also employs a U-Net segmentation network for preprocessing.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results on dermatological image classification using novel ensembling methods. It describes a specific experimental setup involving the ISIC2017 and ISIC2018 datasets, details the use of deep neural networks (VGG16, VGG19, Xception, ResNet50, InceptionV3, DenseNet121) and U-Net segmentation, and reports quantitative performance metrics (ARA, balanced accuracy) comparing ensemble approaches against baseline methods. The structure includes Introduction, Experimental Setup, Results, and Conclusion, which is characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Novel Ensembling Methods for Dermatological Image Classification
brief_summary: This study investigates novel ensembling techniques for dermatological image classification to improve skin lesion detection accuracy. The authors utilized the ISIC2017 and ISIC2018 datasets, employing deep neural networks such as VGG16, VGG19, Xception, ResNet50, InceptionV3, and DenseNet121 alongside U-Net segmentation for preprocessing. The main evaluation approach involved comparing ensemble methods that combine different architectures, hyperparameters, and preprocessing strategies against baseline methods like hyperparameter optimization and test-time augmentation. Key results demonstrated that ensembling models trained with varied preprocessing and hyperparameters significantly outperformed mainstream methods in both speed and accuracy. Specifically, the ensemble achieved an Average ROC AUC of 0.926 on the ISIC2017 dataset and a balanced accuracy of 0.958 on the ISIC2018 dataset. The conclusion highlights that this ensembling strategy provides a considerable boost in performance while reducing the need for extensive hyperparameter tuning.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied in both ISIC2017 (374 training, 30 validation, 117 test images) and ISIC2018 (1113 images) datasets. Used as the positive class for Melanoma ROC AUC (MRA) metric in ISIC2017.
Studied in both ISIC2017 (1372 training, 78 validation, 393 test images) and ISIC2018 (6705 images) datasets. Used as a comparator class in ROC metrics for ISIC2017.
Studied in ISIC2017 (254 training, 42 validation, 90 test images) and ISIC2018 (1099 images). Used as the positive class for Seborrheic keratosis ROC AUC (SRA) metric in ISIC2017.

all_task_types.target: Discrete dermatological lesion categories (e.g., Malignant melanoma, Naevus, Seborrheic Keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on 'dermatological image classification' and 'skin lesion classification'. It uses deep neural networks to predict discrete diagnostic categories from the ISIC2017 (3 classes) and ISIC2018 (7 classes) datasets. Evaluation metrics include Accuracy, Balanced Multiclass Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, and ROC AUC, which are standard for classification tasks. The loss function used is categorical cross entropy.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2017 Dataset
datasets_used.description: Provided by the ISIC2017 challenge organized by The International Society for Digital Imaging of the Skin. Contains images of lesions collected from leading clinical centers internationally and acquired from a variety of devices. Images were varied in size.

use.training: Used for training deep neural networks (VGG16, VGG19, Xception, ResNet50, InceptionV3, DenseNet121) with various hyperparameters and preprocessing strategies (cropping vs original).
use.validation: Used for validation during model training.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluation. Performance was measured using Average ROC AUC (ARA), Melanoma ROC AUC (MRA), and Seborrheic keratosis ROC AUC (SRA).

datasets_used.name: ISIC2018 Dataset (HAM10000)
datasets_used.description: Provided by the ISIC2018 challenge ('Human Against Machine with 10000 training images'). The authors only had access to the labels of the training set and split it into training, validation, and test sets. Contains multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions.

use.training: The original training set (10015 images) was split into 75% training, 20% validation, and 5% test. Used for training models with different batch sizes (3 and 100) and preprocessing (cropped vs original).
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (20% of the original training set).
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluation. Performance was measured using Balanced Multiclass Accuracy.
use.other: The U-Net segmentation network used for preprocessing was trained on images and segmentation masks from Part 1 of the ISIC2018 challenge.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Ranking and comparison of model performance on the ISIC2017 dataset, calculated as the mean of Melanoma ROC AUC and Seborrheic keratosis ROC AUC.
Ranking and comparison of model performance on the ISIC2018 dataset, calculated as the sum of normalized accuracy for each class divided by the number of classes.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve and Area Under the Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Evaluation of classifier performance by measuring the true positive rate as a function of the false positive rate; used to derive specific lesion AUCs (MRA, SRA) and overall ARA.
Visualization and analysis of prediction errors, showing instances where the model predicted the right class versus instances where it confused two specific classes.
Comparison of inference performance by augmenting test images (rotations, flipping, mirroring) and averaging predictions to assess generalization and accuracy improvements.
Comparison of ensemble methods against single models and hyperparameter optimization/test-time augmentation baselines to determine performance boosts in accuracy and speed.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (e.g., Average ROC AUC, Balanced Multiclass Accuracy) and direct numerical comparison of ensemble results against baselines.

Architecture, Image Size and Cropping Im.notes: Cropping helped most with VGG and Xception networks. For 224x224 images, cropping slightly worsened performance for ResNet50, InceptionV3, and DenseNet121.

Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) Impact.notes: Test augmentation improved results slightly for original and cropped images. Improvement increased with more augmented images until 8-16 images.

Ensemble Results.notes: The best score on ISIC2017 was 0.926 ARA, achieved by ensembling all networks (both cropped and original) without test-time augmentations. Ensembling all models without TTA performed better than the best single model with TTA.

Batch Size Ensembling.notes: Ensembling two networks with vastly different batch sizes (3 and 100) always gave a better result than each one individually. Cropped and original ensembles generally gave the best results.

Best Ensemble Configurations.notes: The final score of 0.958 balanced accuracy was achieved by ensembling all models except InceptionV3. This represents an improvement from 80.3% (best non-ensembled) to 95.8% (best ensembled).

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of deep neural networks: VGG16, VGG19, Xception, ResNet50, InceptionV3, DenseNet121, DenseNet169, DenseNet201, and InceptionResNetV2. Additionally, a U-Net segmentation network was used for preprocessing.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer Learning: Keras models were originally trained on the ImageNet dataset to jump-start training.
architecture_used.input_size: Varied by architecture: VGG16/VGG19 (128x128), Xception (160x160), ResNet50/InceptionV3/DenseNet121/169/201/InceptionResNetV2 (224x224). Images were either original or cropped using U-Net segmentation masks.

architecture_details.preprocessing: U-Net segmentation network (open-source implementation) trained on ISIC2018 Part 1 masks to create crop windows. Data augmentation during training: Random rotations [-π, π], Flipping (50%), Mirroring (50%).

classification_or_output_head.type: Softmax (implied by categorical cross entropy and classification task)
classification_or_output_head.output: Class probabilities for dermatological lesion categories (3 classes for ISIC2017, 7 classes for ISIC2018)

preprocessing_techniques: U-Net segmentation network trained on ISIC2018 Part 1 masks to generate segmentation masks for lesion detection
Cropping images using the largest possible rectangle covered by pixels with high confidence from U-Net segmentation masks
Training-time data augmentation: Random rotations (uniform distribution in [-π, π] radians)
Training-time data augmentation: Flipping with 50% probability
Training-time data augmentation: Mirroring with 50% probability
Test-time augmentation (TTA): Creating 1, 2, 4, 8, and 16 sets of augmented images (rotations, flipping, mirroring) and averaging predictions

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training with rotation angles sampled from a uniform distribution in the range [-π, π] radians.
Applied during training with a probability of 50%.
Applied during training with a probability of 50%.

Applied during inference by creating 1, 2, 4, 8, and 16 sets of augmented images (using the same rotation, flipping, and mirroring techniques as training) and averaging the predictions. Improvement plateaued around 8-16 augmented images.

conclusions: Ensembling deep neural networks with different hyperparameters and preprocessing strategies (such as cropping vs. original images) significantly outperforms mainstream methods like hyperparameter optimization and test-time augmentation in both accuracy and speed for skin lesion classification.
The proposed ensembling method achieved an Average ROC AUC of 0.926 on the ISIC2017 dataset by ensembling all networks (both cropped and original) without test-time augmentations.
The method achieved a balanced multiclass accuracy of 0.958 on the ISIC2018 dataset by ensembling all models except InceptionV3.

Ensembling networks trained with vastly different batch sizes (e.g., 3 and 100) consistently yields better results than using either batch size individually, suggesting that hyperparameter tuning can be made obsolete through ensembling.

Test-time augmentation improves individual model performance but does not provide additional benefit when multiple models are already ensembled; in fact, ensembling without test-time augmentation performed better than the best single model with test-time augmentation.

Cropping images using U-Net segmentation masks generally helps performance for smaller image sizes and shallower models (like VGG and Xception) but may slightly worsen performance for larger image sizes (224x224) with deeper networks.

The study demonstrates that combining different preprocessing methods and hyperparameter choices via ensembling provides a considerable boost in accuracy while reducing the need for extensive hyperparameter tuning.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; results are confined to the ISIC2017 and ISIC2018 datasets, limiting generalizability to other clinical settings or imaging devices.

For the ISIC2018 dataset, the authors split the provided training set into training, validation, and test sets (75/20/5) because they only had access to training labels, which may introduce bias compared to the official challenge split.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the performance improvements, relying solely on descriptive metrics.

The ensembling approach increases computational cost and inference latency compared to single models, although the authors argue it is more efficient than extensive hyperparameter optimization or test-time augmentation.

The U-Net segmentation used for preprocessing was trained on Part 1 of the ISIC2018 challenge, which may introduce data leakage or bias if the segmentation masks overlap with the classification task data distribution.
The ISIC2018 dataset is heavily imbalanced (e.g., 6705 Melanocytic nevus vs. 115 Dermatoﬁbroma), which may skew performance metrics despite the use of Balanced Multiclass Accuracy.

The study acknowledges that the cropping technique's benefit is architecture-dependent, helping smaller/shallower models (VGG, Xception) but potentially worsening performance for deeper networks (ResNet, Inception, DenseNet) on 224x224 images.
